SnowDistribution: Wet snowpack. Poor visibility throughout the day above 800 metres. Winds around 30 mph.
Comment: Rock and ice fall will be an additional hazard, particularly overnight.
WeatherInfluences: Increasing winds from the South-South-West become gale force during the day. Rain at all elevations in the evening and overnight turns to snow down to 800 metres in the morning before reverting to rain below 1100 metres in the afternoon. The freezing level drops to 1000 metres in the morning before rising to just above the summits in the afternoon.
SnowStability: Wet snow instabilities will persist mainly on steep North to East aspects above 1000 metres. Stability will be poorest in overnight rain with some improvement in lower temperatures during the day. Cornices will be prone to collapse. Fresh windslab accumulations during the day are not expected to be significant. The avalanche hazard will be Moderate.
ObservedWeatherInfluences: Strong South-Westerly winds and showers falling as rain at all elevations. The freezing level was above the summits.
ObservedSnowStability: The snowpack continues to slowly consolidate in all locations but wet snow instabilities persist mainly on steep North to East aspects above 1000 metres. Cornices are prone to collapse. The avalanche hazard is Moderate.

SnowDistribution: Wet snowpack Diminishing snowpack, good above 900 metres.  Cloud base was at 750 metres all day. Breezy on the tops
Comment: Ice,rock and cornice collapse remains a threat when below crags
WeatherInfluences: Heavy rain at all levels overnight and during the day, with some sleet or snow on the tops for a time. Freezing level mostly well above the summits but briefly at summit levels mid morning. Winds strong to Gale forces South-Westerly.
SnowStability: The thaw continues with more snow loss expected. Some surface instabilities are possible from the steepest slopes above 950m on North-West to Easterly aspects. But generally, although the snowpack will be wet it will remain stable in most areas. Any remaining cornices will weaken further and be prone to collapse. Some potential for minor full depth avalanches from snow slopes and gullies above steep crags.  The avalanche hazard will be Low
ObservedWeatherInfluences: A wet day with rain at all levels easing afternoon.. Mild with summit temperatures around +4 deg C. Winds fresh Southerly on the tops. Poor visibility above 800m.
ObservedSnowStability: The thaw continues with snow loss in most areas however, although moist, stability is generally good in all areas. Greatest snow amounts are mostly above 950m on Northerly to Easterly aspects with ribbons and large patches on other aspects and lower elevations. Avalanche debris noted at base of gullies in main cliffs in Coire Ardair. The avalanche hazard is Low

SnowDistribution: Wet snow throughout. No visibility above 600m. Winds 20-30mph.
Comment: Colder conditions expected for a time on Saturday.
WeatherInfluences: Overnight rain, then dry until late morning when there will be a trace of sleet or wet snow above 1100 metres for a couple of hours. Precipitation will turn to rain at all levels during the afternoon. Fresh South-West winds overnight, backing South-South-Westerly during the day and touching gale force on the highest summits. 1600 metres freezing level overnight, lowering to 1100  metres by mid-morning. Climbing back up to 1400 metres during the afternoon.
SnowStability: The thaw rate will slow for a couple of hours on the highest summits but will continue at lower altitudes, with a generally stable and well bonded snowpack present. Greatest hazard will exist from any remaining cornices which will be prone to collapse. There will also be a risk of ice and rock fall on the very steepest terrain. The avalanche hazard will be Low.
ObservedWeatherInfluences: A mild and damp day with fresh to strong South, veering South-Westerly winds.
ObservedSnowStability: The generally well bonded snowpack is thawing at all levels, with soft, wet surface layers. Greatest cover is present on South-West through North-West to North-East aspects above 800 metres. Remaining cornices are at risk of collapse due to prolonged thaw conditions. Risk of rock and ice fall on the steepest terrain. The avalanche hazard is Low.

SnowDistribution: Wet thawing snowpack. Cloud base was around 600 metres most of the day. Winds 10-20 mph.
Comment: A ridge of high pressure and dry, colder conditions is forecast for Saturday. The remaining snowpack is firm in places and is likely to become very firm on Saturday.
WeatherInfluences: The South-Westerly airflow will continue to bring mild and wet weather. The freezing level will be above the summits with heavy rain and South-South-Westerly winds increasing to reach very strong.
SnowStability: The snowpack is well consolidated and will continue to gradually thaw and diminish. Snowpack stability will be generally good. Reasonable cover will remain on most aspects above around 750 metres, particularly in corries and gullies. The avalanche hazard will be Low.
ObservedWeatherInfluences: Rain at all levels, light in the morning and heavier in the afternoon. The freezing level was well above the summits with moderate South-Westerly winds.
ObservedSnowStability: The remaining snowpack is well consolidated and continues to gradually thaw and diminish. Snowpack stability is generally good. The avalanche hazard is Low.

SnowDistribution: Limited snow cover to lower levels, best above 800 metres.  Poor visibility throughout the day above 800 metres.  Winds 20 -30 mph progress difficult at times with stronger gusts Winds approx 20 mph.
Comment: Wet conditions overnight and for much of Thursday, Remaining generally unsettled with showers or longer spells of rain feeding in from the west. From Friday evening to Saturday evening a transient ridge of high pressure develops, giving a spell of drier weather, although some showers are still possible in the west.
WeatherInfluences: Rain heavy at times at all levels will continue to affect the area, slightly cooler conditions through the day may give a light cover of new snow on the highest tops but amounts are not expected to be significant. Winds will become strong through the day generally from the South-South-West. The freezing level will mainly lie above the Glen Coe summits with a very brief period at around 1000 metres.
SnowStability: Mild conditions with rain will further reduce the snow cover. Generally the snowpack will be soft and moist with reasonable stability. Wet snow instabilities will persist above 950 metres on steep ground in sheltered gullies and on slopes with a North-West through North to Easterly aspect. Gully exits and corrie rims will be most affected. Unstable cornices may also exist in these areas.  The avalanche hazard will be Low.
ObservedWeatherInfluences: Mild conditions with rain  at all levels have affected the area. Winds have been fresh occasionally strong generally South-Westerly. The freezing level is well above the summits.
ObservedSnowStability: The snowpack continues to thaw and diminish particularly at lower levels. The remaining snow is soft wet and is generally stable on all but the steepest slopes where wet snow instabilities exist, most affected areas are sheltered gullies and Northwesterly through North to Easterly slopes mainly above 950 metres.  The avalanche hazard is Low.

SnowDistribution: Thawing snowpack at all altitudes, boulder fields tricky  with potential to sink into deep snow between boulders.  Mostly cloudy on the tops with some bright spells.  Winds 30-40 mph, from mid mountain upwards.
Comment: Cornice collapse, rockfall and wet snow instabilities will be a potential hazard during the mild wet conditions.
WeatherInfluences: Rain, at all levels, this will be heavy at times, and turn to snow just below summit levels mainly during the day. Strong South-Westerly winds will steadily increase to gale force and back to become South-South-Westerly during the day. During the morning the freezing level will fall to around 1100 metres before rising again in the afternoon.
SnowStability: The majority of the snowpack will remain well bonded; however wet snow instabilities will increase, especially overnight on steep North-West to North-East aspects above 950 metres. On these aspects cornices will be prone to collapse. New accumulations of windslab will develop on wind sheltered North to East aspects above 1100 metres, but these will be isolated, shallow and avoidable. The avalanche hazard will be Moderate.
ObservedWeatherInfluences: Mostly dry with spells of rain and drizzle at all levels on strong South-Westerly winds.  The freezing level was well above the summits, with a midday summit temperature of plus 2.6 degrees Celsius.
ObservedSnowStability: The snowpack is soft and thawing at all altitudes. In general the majority of the snowpack is well bonded and stable; however wet snow avalanche and cornice collapse were observed on very steep terrain on a North-West and Northerly aspect above 1000 metres. The avalanche hazard is Low.
